Holly Andres for The New York Times Mariel Hemingway gets up early, perhaps as an unconscious homage to her famous grandfather, to watch the sun rise. Each morning, while still in bed, she and her live- in boyfriend, an erstwhile stuntman and actor named Bobby Williams, begin a series of predawn exercises that consist of breathing, stretching, contemplating the things they\xe2\x80\x99re grateful for and visualizing the day ahead. Hemingway then makes the bed and a pot of jasmine green tea. She fills the hummingbird feeders with organic sugar water, feeds organic soy- free meal to the brood of egg- laying hens that live in her backyard and heads back to the kitchen to prepare a smoothie. At this point in their elaborate morning ritual, on an overcast day in early May, I joined Hemingway and Williams. They live in a modest three- bedroom ranch house hidden behind a giant oak tree, at the end of a sinuous maze of gravelly roads, deep in a canyon on the edge of Malibu. At quarter to 6, Hemingway was in the kitchen steeping her second pot of green tea while talking about a recent dinner with Woody Allen, who directed her Oscar- nominated performance in \xe2\x80\x9cManhattan\xe2\x80\x9d their first meeting in 15 years. At 51, Hemingway still bears an uncanny resemblance to the 17- year- old girl she portrayed in that film: she has the same long, athletic limbs; the cliff- jump cheekbones; the high, distinctive, Muppet voice. But whereas her character in \xe2\x80\x9cManhattan\xe2\x80\x9d was unnaturally poised and still, Hemingway is loose, unguarded and disarmingly funny. Williams padded into the room, still blurry with sleep. Shirtless and in yoga pants, he displayed the chiseled muscles of someone for whom working out is a primary occupation. (In the 1990s, he was the guy exercising in the Soloflex commercial.) Although he is 50, there is much about him, from the diminutive \xe2\x80\x9cBobby\xe2\x80\x9d to his mop of brown hair, that seems boyish. His manner, however, can be tightly coiled. \xe2\x80\x9cO.K., first of all, let\xe2\x80\x99s tell the truth,\xe2\x80\x9d he said. \xe2\x80\x9cYou wouldn\xe2\x80\x99t even hear me breathing in the morning. You\xe2\x80\x99re here, so Mariel\xe2\x80\x99s giving you stories, but she doesn\xe2\x80\x99t talk at all.\xe2\x80\x9d \xe2\x80\x9c You don\xe2\x80\x99t say a word,\xe2\x80\x9d Hemingway said. \xe2\x80\x9cDon\xe2\x80\x99t throw me under the bus!\xe2\x80\x9d She laughed. In silence, Williams, whom Hemingway calls her \xe2\x80\x9clife partner\xe2\x80\x9d they have been together for four and a half years began loading ingredients into a blender: avocados, coconut water, dates, superfood powder and various herbal tinctures. Hemingway narrated over the grinding. \xe2\x80\x9cWe put in turmeric for inflammation . . . cinnamon for metabolism and blood . . . the dates are for the thyroid, plus the sweetness is nice. . . . We don\xe2\x80\x99t put fruit in it because it changes it from being an alkaline thing to being more acidic.\xe2\x80\x9d Hemingway, who has written a yoga memoir, an organic- foods cookbook and two self- help books (one written with Williams), believes that the quotidian decisions we make, from the foods we consume to the amount of time we spend lollygagging on the Internet what she calls our \xe2\x80\x9clifestyle choices\xe2\x80\x9d have a profound impact on mood and well- being. She has maintained this for years, long before the idea became mainstream. \xe2\x80\x9cI definitely grew up the healthiest person on the planet,\xe2\x80\x9d her daughter, Langley Fox, told me. \xe2\x80\x9cMy first \xe2\x80\x98cookie\xe2\x80\x99 was a nonflavored rice cracker. How a healthy lifestyle will help your whole well- being I got the whole spiel.\xe2\x80\x9d It\xe2\x80\x99s a topic Hemingway has thought a lot about, because her family not only has a celebrated artistic legacy but also a darker psychological one. Her grandfather, the writer Ernest Hemingway, was a notorious heavy drinker who suffered frequent bouts of depression and committed suicide by shooting himself in the head with a 12- gauge double- barreled shotgun at age 61, four months before Mariel was born. Her older sister Margaux, one of the highest- paid models of the 1970s, struggled with drug addiction, bulimia and alcohol- induced epilepsy; at 41, on the day before the 35th anniversary of her grandfather\xe2\x80\x99s death, she intentionally overdosed on phenobarbital. Her oldest sister, Muffet, is bipolar and schizophrenic and has been in and out of institutions much of her life. And that\xe2\x80\x99s just her immediate family. Ernest\xe2\x80\x99s father, her great- grandfather, also killed himself, as did his brother, his sister and his first wife\xe2\x80\x99s father, Mariel\xe2\x80\x99s other great- grandfather. (It\xe2\x80\x99s suspected that another Hemingway sister might also have taken her own life, at age 65, though her doctors claimed natural causes.) In his book, \xe2\x80\x9cStrange Tribe: A Family Memoir,\xe2\x80\x9d John Patrick Hemingway, the son of Ernest\xe2\x80\x99s youngest son, Gregory, called suicide \xe2\x80\x9cthe family exit.\xe2\x80\x9d The term \xe2\x80\x9cHemingway curse\xe2\x80\x9d has become shorthand for the tangle of mental illness, addiction and suicide that has plagued multiple generations. This family tree, as blood- soaked as any from Greek tragedy, is explored in \xe2\x80\x9cRunning From Crazy,\xe2\x80\x9d a riveting new documentary directed and produced by the Academy Award- winning director Barbara Kopple. The film, shown to great acclaim at Sundance, has its premiere in theaters Nov. 1 and will appear on OWN in 2014. (Oprah Winfrey is an executive producer on the film.) The title is an apt summation of the film\xe2\x80\x99s premise: Hemingway has spent her life outrunning the \xe2\x80\x9ccrazy\xe2\x80\x9d that seemed to be her genetic inheritance. \xe2\x80\x9cWhen Margaux died, I thought, Oh, no, it\xe2\x80\x99s my turn. I\xe2\x80\x99m going to get the sickness,\xe2\x80\x9d Hemingway told me. \xe2\x80\x9cHonestly, I thought I could catch it.\xe2\x80\x9d She has built a life of preventive measures. The morning I visited, we all piled into the car, a white Mini Cooper with a spider- web crack fanning across the windshield, to head to one of the couple\xe2\x80\x99s \xe2\x80\x9csunrise spots\xe2\x80\x9d in the Santa Monica Mountains. They believe that gazing at the day\xe2\x80\x99s first light benefits the endocrine and immune systems. Williams, who was driving, tossed off a litany of health and wellness- related theories and statistics. \xe2\x80\x9cMariel and I, our idealistic vision is, Why have sickness? Why not just get rid of it? Why not educate our kids to go outside, breathe air?\xe2\x80\x9d He talked fast, his Long Island accent growing more evident with every word. I wondered about the superfood powder they put in the smoothie. \xe2\x80\x9cI talk about the six doctors,\xe2\x80\x9d he continued, \xe2\x80\x9cDr. Sun, Dr. Air, Dr. Water, Dr. Nutrition, Dr. Exercise and Dr. Rest. You want to take your guys from the A.M.A. and have them compete against my guys? \xe2\x80\x99Cause my guys will give your guys a run for their money!\xe2\x80\x9d Hemingway, who was holding his hand, gave him the indulgent look people in relationships give each other and said wryly, \xe2\x80\x9cHe\xe2\x80\x99s not opinionated at all, as you can see.\xe2\x80\x9d By 6:15, we reached the rocky precipice, but a layer of fog was shrouding the sun. Hemingway and Williams slipped off their sneakers going barefoot whenever possible is another of their beliefs and climbed 20 feet or so up the orange- red rock, their practiced, agile toes gripping its crags like fingers. \xe2\x80\x9cYour skin is the largest organ,\xe2\x80\x9d Williams said, \xe2\x80\x9cand 70 percent of the absorption is through the bottom of your feet. Think they\xe2\x80\x99re important? And you have shoes on! What are you absorbing now? Zero! What am I absorbing? Minerals, the earth!\xe2\x80\x9d Hemingway crouched on the rock and looked out into the distance. In the contemplative quiet, you could hear the roar of the ocean far below. Williams broke the silence: \xe2\x80\x9cMy dad always says to me, \xe2\x80\x98What do you do all day if you don\xe2\x80\x99t work?\xe2\x80\x99 I say: \xe2\x80\x98Aw, Dad, there\xe2\x80\x99s not enough hours in the day for me to take care of myself! Do you know how much stuff I have to do?\xe2\x80\x99 \xe2\x80\x9d I pointed out that their joint self- care regimen is something of a full- time job. Hemingway nodded. \xe2\x80\x9cWe looked at each other one day, and we were like, \xe2\x80\x98How can we turn how we love to live into our job?\xe2\x80\x99 Why not turn it into helping other people live a better life? What I love about our book and our message is that it\xe2\x80\x99s completely doable,\xe2\x80\x9d she said. \xe2\x80\x9cIt\xe2\x80\x99s not like this is so hard. Everybody can watch the sun.\xe2\x80\x9d \xe2\x80\x9cRunning From Crazy\xe2\x80\x9d follows the three sisters born to Jack Hemingway, the son of Ernest and Hadley, who was the first of his four wives. Mariel, the youngest of the three girls, was an unexpected addition born 11 years after Joan, or Muffet, and nearly seven years after Margaux, who was so jealous of her baby sister that she cut off Mariel\xe2\x80\x99s eyelashes with a pair of scissors. Their home life was gloomy and volatile. Jack and his beautiful but resentful wife, Byra (known as Puck), fought a lot and drank even more. Their nightly cocktail hours, which they called \xe2\x80\x9cwine time,\xe2\x80\x9d escalated into arguments that ended in broken glass and blood. Mariel, who from an early age defined herself as the good, obedient daughter, wiped up the mess. Her sisters, meanwhile, ran wild. In her memoir, \xe2\x80\x9cFinding My Balance,\xe2\x80\x9d Hemingway remembers Margaux, rebellious and gorgeous, frequenting the local bars of Ketchum, Idaho, at age 14, then \xe2\x80\x9cfearlessly bolting down double black- diamond runs stoned and drunk.\xe2\x80\x9d Muffet\xe2\x80\x99s prolific LSD use triggered a full- blown psychosis that sent her tearing naked through the streets of their small town and threatening to stab her mother before she was finally institutionalized. Hemingway and Bobby Williams at home in Malibu, Calif. Credit... Holly Andres for The New York Times Another blow arrived when Puck developed a cancerous tumor on her thymus gland. Hemingway, at age 11, became her mother\xe2\x80\x99s caretaker and companion, accompanying her on the three- hour drive to her radiation and chemotherapy appointments in Boise and sleeping with her at night. Her father retreated into himself and the basement room Hemingway calls his \xe2\x80\x9cland of seclusion.\xe2\x80\x9d You could not have devised a better experiment for driving someone insane. And yet Hemingway, who is the voice and conscience not only of the film but also of her family as a whole, comes across as solid, intelligent and resilient. \xe2\x80\x9cMariel has always been different,\xe2\x80\x9d Woody Allen says. \xe2\x80\x9cShe\xe2\x80\x99s the one that had the personal resources and the human qualities to survive all that terrible onus.\xe2\x80\x9d Most remarkable of all is her penetrating candor, which seems courageous, if possibly inadvisable at times. (\xe2\x80\x9cSometimes I would say, \xe2\x80\x98O.K., I don\xe2\x80\x99t need to know all that!\xe2\x80\x99 \xe2\x80\x9d Barbara Kopple told me, laughing.) Her honesty may also be an act of rebellion: Hemingway says her father never spoke about Ernest or the mental illness on his side of the family for years, his daughters believed that their grandfather shot himself accidently while cleaning his gun. Among the most moving moments of the film are those in which Hemingway talks honestly with her two daughters about their shared history something she had previously been unable to do. \xe2\x80\x9cI really haven\xe2\x80\x99t told you that much about the family history of all the suicide stuff, because I don\xe2\x80\x99t think that I ever wanted you to feel burdened by it,\xe2\x80\x9d she says to Langley, a slight girl with dark, soulful eyes, as they sit together after a suicide- prevention walk. Her sense of urgency about breaking the curse has as much to do with the next generation of Hemingways as it does with her own personal happiness. \xe2\x80\x9cRunning From Crazy\xe2\x80\x9d makes masterly use of raw archival footage taken by Margaux while filming her own documentary about her grandfather in 1983. The footage adds a powerful second layer to the film. As Margaux retraces Ernest\xe2\x80\x99s life in Paris, Spain and Ketchum, we not only witness the walking, talking reality of her parents and sister, Muffet, laughing with one another and enjoying their \xe2\x80\x9cwine time,\xe2\x80\x9d but we also get a close- up of her pain, which is palpable and heartbreaking. Margaux is no longer the sylphlike young woman adored by the camera. Instead, we see a heavier, altogether more lugubrious and fragile- seeming person. Her hair is thin and strawlike; her strange, low, scratchy voice is slurry and indistinct: you can almost hear the addiction in it. In one eerie clip, she stands in front of the house where Ernest shot himself and says, \xe2\x80\x9cI\xe2\x80\x99ve always felt that if somebody can\xe2\x80\x99t go on living and creating the way they can, I mean, the way they used to, and in a healthy form, in which Grandpapa was accustomed to . . . I mean, I accept the fact that he . . . that he killed himself.\xe2\x80\x9d At the time this footage was filmed, Margaux\xe2\x80\x99s film career had foundered, while Mariel\xe2\x80\x99s had taken off. This was especially galling to the older Hemingway, because she had procured her sister her first role, in the 1976 rape- revenge fantasy, \xe2\x80\x9cLipstick.\xe2\x80\x9d Vincent Canby, writing in The New York Times, dismissed \xe2\x80\x9cLipstick\xe2\x80\x9d as \xe2\x80\x9canti- intellectual in the ways that B movies always have been\xe2\x80\x9d and Margaux as \xe2\x80\x9cnot much of an actress yet.\xe2\x80\x9d Meanwhile, he wrote that Mariel \xe2\x80\x9cgives an immensely moving, utterly unaffected performance that shows up everything else as a calculated swindle.\xe2\x80\x9d Impressed by \xe2\x80\x9cLipstick,\xe2\x80\x9d Woody Allen cast Mariel in \xe2\x80\x9cManhattan\xe2\x80\x9d as Tracy, the wise 17- year- old girlfriend of his neurotic 42- year- old alter ego, Isaac. She was 16 at the time and had never had a boyfriend; Woody Allen was her first real kiss. She remembers the making of the film fondly. She stayed at the apartment of Ernest\xe2\x80\x99s fourth wife, Mary, and Allen, she says, took an avuncular interest in her, taking her to museums and movies. \xe2\x80\x9cIt made me feel mature,\xe2\x80\x9d she remembers. \xe2\x80\x9cIt made me feel seen. I was such a kid . . . and he was really kind.\xe2\x80\x9d Hemingway dropped out of school and moved to New York, partly to pursue acting full time and partly to get a break from taking care of her mother at home. (Her mother died in 1988, when Mariel was 26; she was ill, more or less, for 15 years.) Hemingway says now that she wishes her parents had insisted that she remain in Idaho. Once she was on her own, her need for control intensified. She cast herself as the self- contained foil to her sister\xe2\x80\x99s barely contained chaos. \xe2\x80\x9cI was like, I can control my life, why can\xe2\x80\x99t she control her life?\xe2\x80\x9d she says of Margaux, who, in the ensuing years, would marry and divorce twice, declare bankruptcy, flicker in and out of sobriety and do a stint at the Betty Ford Center. Hemingway\xe2\x80\x99s self- discipline expressed itself mainly as a rigid attitude toward food. She was vegetarian, then vegan; she ate all fruit, no fat, very little protein; she drank only espresso while fasting for days, \xe2\x80\x9caddicted to the feeling of lightness.\xe2\x80\x9d Eventually, her thyroid shut down, and her periods stopped, the latter of which pleased her: \xe2\x80\x9cIn not allowing my female qualities to come fully into bloom, I thought I was controlling my own health and sanity,\xe2\x80\x9d she writes. \xe2\x80\x9cMy female role models represented nothing I wanted illness, instability and heaviness.\xe2\x80\x9d Her boyish figure and natural athleticism led to a starring role as an Olympic track- team hopeful in Robert Towne\xe2\x80\x99s \xe2\x80\x9cPersonal Best.\xe2\x80\x9d Next came Bob Fosse\xe2\x80\x99s \xe2\x80\x9cStar 80,\xe2\x80\x9d in which Hemingway played Dorothy Stratten, the Playboy playmate murdered by her estranged husband. Because Fosse was not immediately convinced that the prepubescent girl- boy of \xe2\x80\x9cPersonal Best\xe2\x80\x9d could play a voluptuous sex symbol, Hemingway got breast implants and lobbied hard for the role, explaining that she understood Stratten\xe2\x80\x99s desperate need to be loved. Despite the relative critical success of \xe2\x80\x9cStar 80,\xe2\x80\x9d Hemingway\xe2\x80\x99s career stalled in the wake of its release. The following year, in 1984, she married Stephen Crisman, who at the time was 34 and the manager of the Hard Rock Cafe in New York. Within five years she had given birth to their two daughters, Dree, now 25, and a model, and Langley, 24, an illustrator. Hemingway continued to work regularly, if not always illustriously, taking on a role a year, often for financial reasons. (She doesn\xe2\x80\x99t receive money from the Hemingway estate.) \xe2\x80\x9cI was focused on being a mother. I\xe2\x80\x99d do something to make money, to support the family. . . . I\xe2\x80\x99d get offered a ton of bad movies, and I did some of them, just to get by,\xe2\x80\x9d she says. For her, those years were marred by anxiety, obsessiveness and depression. \xe2\x80\x9cIt was kind of like a low- grade fever,\xe2\x80\x9d she says. Her career wasn\xe2\x80\x99t absorbing; her marriage was unhappy; she was self- destructively healthy. Her diet became even more abstemious, consisting of \xe2\x80\x9cbig bowls of salad or popcorn or these big foamy things of coffee.\xe2\x80\x9d (She would put organic instant coffee in a blender with hot water, blend it with ice and eat the foam an anorexic\xe2\x80\x99s cappuccino.) She exercised two, three or four hours a day, until she would become sick and \xe2\x80\x9cbroken down.\xe2\x80\x9d She sought out \xe2\x80\x9ca succession of strange spiritual wacks, psychics, astrologers and holistic doctors\xe2\x80\x9d to free her from her obsessions. There was one period during which she felt her family would be better off without her. In 1996, when Margaux took her own life, Hemingway, who had recently begun to repair her relationship with her sister, was shocked. \xe2\x80\x9cI was sad that I wasn\xe2\x80\x99t as sad as I probably should have been,\xe2\x80\x9d Hemingway told me, with signature frankness. \xe2\x80\x9cI didn\xe2\x80\x99t know how to feel. It was so confusing. It was so sad.\xe2\x80\x9d Three years later, Hemingway\xe2\x80\x99s husband, Stephen Crisman, received a diagnosis of Stage 4 melanoma, which thrust her back into the role of caretaker. (The couple divorced in 2008, several years after Crisman\xe2\x80\x99s cancer went into remission.) \xe2\x80\x9cMy days were about getting through,\xe2\x80\x9d Hemingway says. \xe2\x80\x9cHow do I manage myself and not end up like the members of my family? How do I control what I\xe2\x80\x99m feeling?\xe2\x80\x9d Although her depression has lifted, Hemingway is still like a sentry guarding her self. In the months I trailed her, I never witnessed her indulge in a single unhealthful habit. She abstains from alcohol, has renounced coffee, forgoes gluten and sugar and appears to subsist mainly on avocados, almonds, eggs and large quantities of salad greens. She believes her way of living has kept her sane and alive. Her most recent book, \xe2\x80\x9c Running With Nature ,\xe2\x80\x9d written with Williams and published in June, is essentially a chronicle of a healthful lifestyle perfected. \xe2\x80\x9cIt\xe2\x80\x99s an experiment in how to live,\xe2\x80\x9d Hemingway says. The couple\xe2\x80\x99s 1.5- acre property, which they call the Ranch, serves as their laboratory. There are organic vegetable beds, an infrared sauna and, in the backyard, an adult playground of sorts, featuring parallel bars, punching bags, a slackline, a rope ladder, a climbing wall, a basketball court, a full gym, a giant trampoline and a tepee making time for \xe2\x80\x9cplay\xe2\x80\x9d is one of the main principles of the book. While some of their suggestions (holistic dentists, tossing your microwave because it renders food carcinogenic) may seem extreme to anyone unfamiliar with alternative- health dogma, most of them (going outside, getting more sleep) are common knowledge. A week after our early- morning hike, I accompanied Hemingway to an evening event at the ArcLight, a movie theater in Hollywood. A 10- minute video she stars in for the Los Angeles County Department of Mental Health\xe2\x80\x99s Emmy- winning \xe2\x80\x9cProfiles of Hope\xe2\x80\x9d series was being shown, as were some clips from \xe2\x80\x9cRunning From Crazy.\xe2\x80\x9d In the past few years, Hemingway has become a regular figure on the mental- health- awareness circuit, speaking for organizations like McLean Hospital and the American Foundation for Suicide Prevention. \xe2\x80\x9cPeople feel like, \xe2\x80\x98O.K., if this celebrity can do this, I can, too,\xe2\x80\x99 \xe2\x80\x9d Robin Kay, chief deputy director for the Los Angeles County Department of Mental Health, told me, as we waited for the event to begin. In this clip from the coming documentary, Margaux and Mariel Hemingway revisit the home where their grandfather, Ernest, committed suicide. \xe2\x80\x9cAll I want to do is to inspire others to say, \xe2\x80\x98It\xe2\x80\x99s O.K., I\xe2\x80\x99m not alone, I can tell my story,\xe2\x80\x99 \xe2\x80\x9d Hemingway told the audience, gesturing emphatically. \xe2\x80\x9cThere is mental illness and mental instability practically in everybody\xe2\x80\x99s life.\xe2\x80\x9d Her message was one of equality, of our shared human journey. \xe2\x80\x9cOf course, I have your story,\xe2\x80\x9d she told me in the car on the way home. \xe2\x80\x9cIt\xe2\x80\x99s everybody\xe2\x80\x99s story. Everybody has the same story.\xe2\x80\x9d In telling her story, she spares no detail: her sister\xe2\x80\x99s suicide, her simmering depression, the bizarre diets and \xe2\x80\x9cwackadoo\xe2\x80\x9d gurus. She cries as easily as she laughs, her eyes filling and her voice wobbling as she speaks. Her emotions are right on the surface; witnessing them come and go is like watching clouds gather and pass. The audience listened, rapt. Many of those who came to see her that night had personal experience with suicide: they\xe2\x80\x99d lost a family member, or they\xe2\x80\x99d attempted suicide themselves. Hemingway gently pointed out that unlike almost any other illness \xe2\x80\x9cAIDS or addiction or alcoholism or diabetes\xe2\x80\x9d mental illness and suicide remain taboo topics of discussion. \xe2\x80\x9cPeople feel tremendous shame around it,\xe2\x80\x9d she said. The theater darkened, and the \xe2\x80\x9cProfiles of Hope\xe2\x80\x9d video began. There was Hemingway, all blond hair and tawny skin, seated in her sunlit living room. \xe2\x80\x9cI truly feel I have broken the chain of whatever that is people have called it the family curse,\xe2\x80\x9d she says. As empowering as this idea is, it\xe2\x80\x99s also potentially controversial, because she often speaks to practitioners who take a more traditional meds- and- talk- therapy approach. She is careful to present her methods as only one possible solution. And she concedes that some people need antidepressants: her sister, Muffet, she says, is on a \xe2\x80\x9ctruckload of pharmaceuticals.\xe2\x80\x9d In the video, Hemingway enumerated ways of healing yourself. She advised viewers to \xe2\x80\x9cbe a kid again, play like you\xe2\x80\x99re a child.\xe2\x80\x9d There were images of Hemingway walking the slackline and scaling the rope ladder in her backyard. \xe2\x80\x9cWe\xe2\x80\x99ve got a climbing wall,\xe2\x80\x9d she narrated. \xe2\x80\x9cWe\xe2\x80\x99ve got stationary bikes. We\xe2\x80\x99ve got organic biodynamic gardens.\xe2\x80\x9d At the mention of organic biodynamic gardens, I heard two women sitting to my right snicker. When the lights came on, I turned to them. One of the women told me she was a real estate agent who specialized in time- share properties, a sector of the market no longer flourishing. In the past two years, she\xe2\x80\x99d also developed spinal stenosis and arthritis in her neck that caused her chronic pain. \xe2\x80\x9cI lost my house, and I lost my rental properties, and I lost my job,\xe2\x80\x9d she said, standing with one foot in front of the other, as if to steady herself, \xe2\x80\x9cand the rug from my life was just pulled out from underneath me . . . and I went into a really superdeep depression.\xe2\x80\x9d I asked what she thought of the clips. \xe2\x80\x9cI wish I had that kind of lifestyle,\xe2\x80\x9d she said, \xe2\x80\x9cbut so many things limit me from having that, and that\xe2\x80\x99s an excuse, yes, but it\xe2\x80\x99s a really good one it\xe2\x80\x99s a reality. I mean, I don\xe2\x80\x99t live in a beautiful place and have that setting as my backyard. I don\xe2\x80\x99t have a life partner who supports me. . . .\xe2\x80\x9d \xe2\x80\x9cSunday is play day!\xe2\x80\x9d Hemingway said, in her singsong Jim Henson- character voice. She and Williams and I were driving 60 miles up the Pacific Coast Highway to Ojai, Calif., where they would shop for sunflower sprouts, pumpkin seeds and raw sprouted almonds at the local farmers\xe2\x80\x99 market and visit a healer who used dowsing rods to make his diagnoses. (\xe2\x80\x9cBobby,\xe2\x80\x9d said the healer, a stern but solicitous middle- aged man in a white lab coat, with a Slavic accent so thick as to render him nearly unintelligible, \xe2\x80\x9cneeds to eat fermented foods to purify his liver and deal with his anger.\xe2\x80\x9d Hemingway was \xe2\x80\x9cbalanced.\xe2\x80\x9d I was to increase \xe2\x80\x9cmy appetites\xe2\x80\x9d by smoking pot.) The freeway was a thick soup of Sunday afternoon beach traffic, but Williams would not be deterred. He darted back and forth between lanes, exploiting any visible space between cars to wedge in. When this failed to move us ahead with satisfactory swiftness, he swerved to the right shoulder and drove 80 miles an hour on it the rest of the way, jerking the car sharply back into the lane whenever the shoulder ended. \xe2\x80\x9cPlease be watchful for the Man, babe,\xe2\x80\x9d Hemingway said, warning him to look out for cops, though she did not seem all that bothered by the speed. \xe2\x80\x9cShe definitely has that Hemingway gene of living on the edge, of allowing adrenaline to come through,\xe2\x80\x9d Kopple later told me. As we zoomed along, Williams lectured me through the rearview mirror on the benefits of raw egg yolk and buffalo meat. \xe2\x80\x9cThe stronger the animal, it\xe2\x80\x99s going to be energetically better for you,\xe2\x80\x9d he said. In the mirror, I could see the words \xe2\x80\x9cJust Breathe\xe2\x80\x9d written on his T- shirt. I tried to remember this as he revved to 115 miles an hour. I was reminded of one of the documentary\xe2\x80\x99s climactic scenes: Williams is driving on a dirt road in the middle of the Idaho desert when he grabs the emergency brake in an attempt to spin the car around action- flick style, which causes it to stall. This disrupts the mountain- climbing expedition they\xe2\x80\x99d planned and creates a crisis over how to deal with the car. When Hemingway starts to get upset, Williams tries to shut her down: \xe2\x80\x9cLook, you can\xe2\x80\x99t talk like a girl. Be a boy for one minute. Just one minute. No girl talk.\xe2\x80\x9d The argument that ensues is not exactly an advertisement for healthful living. But the scene ends, astonishingly, with a quiet Hemingway scaling a mountain nonetheless. \xe2\x80\x9cBobby brought spontaneity into my life,\xe2\x80\x9d Hemingway said, back on the road to Ojai. \xe2\x80\x9cI realize now I was really rigid. I was always about order and routine.\xe2\x80\x9d She swiveled around to face me in the back seat. \xe2\x80\x9cA little of that is good, but too much of that makes you old I was older when I met him.\xe2\x80\x9d On the way home, fed up with traffic, the pair pulled over on the side of the road to climb a massive sand dune. Upon reaching the summit, they began to perform joint calisthenics. From my vantage at the bottom of the dune, they were miniature figurines on the top of a wedding cake. Miniature Williams got down in the sand and did push- ups. Miniature Hemingway followed. They did stomach crunches. Miniature Williams heaved a boulder in the air and started lifting it up and down. Miniature Hemingway lifted two smaller rocks like free weights. As I watched them, it occurred to me that Williams is a contemporary, extreme- sports version of Ernest Hemingway, without the literary ambitions. He is all of his masculine pretensions distilled. Another day, when Mariel and I dropped by the house after an interview to find him standing at the counter in a cowboy hat making a gluten- free pizza, he said: \xe2\x80\x9cHemingway was a show- off! He was trying to be something he wasn\xe2\x80\x99t! He had no confidence! If I got him in a boxing ring, I\xe2\x80\x99d give him a whupping.\xe2\x80\x9d Mariel hooted with laughter, put her hand over his mouth and said: \xe2\x80\x9cBe quiet. I love you, but you\xe2\x80\x99ll sound stupid.\xe2\x80\x9d When I last spoke to Hemingway in early October, she told me she was rereading her grandfather\xe2\x80\x99s books, as she does every few years. It makes her feel close to him. She also plans to produce the film version of \xe2\x80\x9cA Moveable Feast,\xe2\x80\x9d the story of her grandparents\xe2\x80\x99 relationship in Paris and her favorite book. She has purchased the option from the estate. \xe2\x80\x9cIt represents a piece of me,\xe2\x80\x9d she says. \xe2\x80\x9cI\xe2\x80\x99m a part of it in a way, because my father was a baby then.\xe2\x80\x9d As she talked about Ernest\xe2\x80\x99s work, it became clear that a tendency toward depression isn\xe2\x80\x99t the only thing she inherited from her grandfather. There is also, arguably, a more positive connection, a shared quest: \xe2\x80\x9cThe machismo thing, I think that\xe2\x80\x99s secondary,\xe2\x80\x9d she said. \xe2\x80\x9cI think it\xe2\x80\x99s a cover- up for what he was really doing, which is looking at deep emotion. To me, that means being truthful about what is real and what is happening. It\xe2\x80\x99s being true to what is. That\xe2\x80\x99s why he loved adventurous circumstances, because they bring out raw emotion: there\xe2\x80\x99s no bull in the middle of a bullfight.\xe2\x80\x9d Amanda Fortini is a writer who lives in Livingston, Mont.
